coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots used as an “on–off–on” fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ and glutathione detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Yan
Xu, Lingxia
Tang, Ke
Guan, Yutong
Wang, Ting
Wang, Hua
Yu, William W.
coredata.dc:description: A novel kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with strong blue fluorescence emission was synthesized via microwave pyrolysis method using humic acid, urea, and citric acid as the precursor. We found that the fluorescence emission of N-CDs can be firstly quenched by Fe3+ (turn-off) and then recovered by glutathione (turn-on). Moreover, N-CDs applied as an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and glutathione (GSH) was further studied in this work. N-CDs showed good selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ than other metal ions, and the limit of detection (LoD) is lower to 0.102 μM. Furthermore, the stable N-CDs/Fe3+exhibited a high sensitivity detection for GSH with a LoD of 0.226 μM. In addition, density functional theory calculations were further performed to reveal the mechanism of the “turn-off” and “turn-on” process. According to geometries and frontier molecular orbital (FMO) results, N-CDs exhibited strong interaction with Fe3+ rather than other metal ions, and the N-CDs/Fe3+ complex exhibited the highest stability among other complexes. Furthermore, this “on–off–on” fluorescent sensor was successfully applied as a stain-screening detector for Streptococcus thermophilus with high production of GSH.
coredata.dcterms:subject: On-off-on” fluorescent sensor
Computational analysis
Glutathione detection
Strain-screening detector
Streptococcus thermophilus strain

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence selectivity of N-CDs towards Fe3+ and GSH
Fluorescence detection of GSH produced by S. thermophilus
Fluorescent selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+
Theoretical analysis for the selectivity of N-CDs towards metal ions
Application of the “on–off–on” sensor in the GSH-producing capability detection of S. thermophilus

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-CDs (inset represents diameter distribution of N-CDs); (b) High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of N-CDs; (c) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs; (d) XPS spectrum of N-CDs; high-resolution XPS spectra of (e) C 1s, (f) N 1s, and (g) O 1s; (h) Absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs; (i) fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) Selectivity of N-CDs (0.2 g/L) solution for Fe3+ (10−3 M) over other metal ions (10−3 M); (b) Fluorescence response of N-CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+.
(a) Selectivity of N-CDs/Fe3+ towards different kinds of amino acid (10−2 M); (b) Fluorescence response of the N-CDs/Fe3+system towards GSH.
Optimized geometries of N-CDs/Mn+ complexes along with the key bond distances (in Å).
Isosurfaces of HOMO-1, HOMO, LUMO, LUMO+1 and LUMO+2 of N-CDs/Mn+ complexes.
Schematic of the N-CDs as an “on–off–on” fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and GSH.

(a) Fluorescence recovery degree of N-CDs/Fe3+ with different kinds of S. thermophilus strain (the insets are photo images of the corresponding samples under 365 nm UV lamp); (b) N-CDs (1), N-CDs/Fe3+ (2), and N-CDs/Fe3+ inoculated by 100 μL of S. thermophilus strain in liquid medium under 365 nm UV lamp; (c) PL spectra of 1, 2, and 3 in liquid medium; (d) Photo images of 1, 2, and 3 in solid medium under 365 nm UV lamp.
Different fluorescent sensors reported for Fe3+ detection.
The methods reported in literatures for GSH detection.
Calculated binding energies (BEs), metal ion charge distributions for surface adsorbed N-CDs/Mn+ complexes, and Charge transfer from N-CDs to Mn+ (a.u.).

row.entry: Charge transfer from N-CDs to Mn+ (a.u.)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Parameters in parentheses are calculated charges transfer from N-CDs to metal ions.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots used as an “on–off–on” fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ and glutathione detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking
Qilu University, of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with strong blue fluorescence emission was synthesized via microwave pyrolysis method using humic acid, urea, and citric acid as the precursor. We found that the fluorescence emission of N-CDs can be firstly quenched by Fe3+ (turn-off) and then recovered by glutathione (turn-on). Moreover, N-CDs applied as an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and glutathione (GSH) was further studied in this work. N-CDs showed good selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ than other metal ions, and the limit of detection (LoD) is lower to 0.102 μM. Furthermore, the stable N-CDs/Fe3+exhibited a high sensitivity detection for GSH with a LoD of 0.226 μM. In addition, density functional theory calculations were further performed to reveal the mechanism of the “turn-off” and “turn-on” process. According to geometries and frontier molecular orbital (FMO) results, N-CDs exhibited strong interaction with Fe3+ rather than other metal ions, and the N-CDs/Fe3+ complex exhibited the highest stability among other complexes. Furthermore, this “on–off–on” fluorescent sensor was successfully applied as a stain-screening detector for Streptococcus thermophilus with high production of GSH.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) recently appeared as a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials due to their facile preparation, excellent fluorescent properties, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high photostability [1,2]. They have been widely applied in biological labeling, drug delivery, light-emitting diodes and fluorescent detection [3]. In these decades, many efforts were focus on preparing CDs in different ways, including hydrothermal, electrochemical, microwave pyrolysis and so on [4]. Among these methods, microwave pyrolysis attracted much attention due to short reaction time, high product yield and convenience [5]. Besides, in order to achieve CDs with excellent optical property and high fluorescence quantum yield, heteroatoms (N, S, B, P) doped CDs emerged and generated significant research interest owing to the presence of new functional sites [6–8]. Among these doped CDs, nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) is dominant for long emission wavelengths, high fluorescent quantum yields, and other unique fluorescent behaviors and is beneficial to be widely applying [9].

In most cases, the detection principle of CDs as a fluorescent sensor was the “turn-off” or “turn on” process of the CDs fluorescence emission towards analytes, corresponding to the fluorescence quenching or recovery. The single “turn-off” process is more easily influenced by other parameters and presenting in false positives [10]. Therefore, a novel kind of fluorescent sensors based on “on-off-on” process was developed and exhibited great potential for metal ion detection [11]. Generally, the fluorescence emission of the “on-off-on” CD fluorescent sensors is firstly quenched by high valence metal ions and then recovered by other analytes like reducing substances [12,13]. Luo et al. reported an “on-off-on” sensor based on N-CDs/Fe3+/AA with simultaneous response towards Fe3+ and AA. The author demonstrated that fluorescence recovery of N,S-CDs could be ascribed to the redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA. As the interaction between N,S-CDs/Fe2+ was weaker than N,S-CDs/Fe3+, the fluorescent emission of N,S-CDs can be partly restored when Fe3+ was reduced to Fe2+ by AA [11].

Reduced glutathione (GSH) plays a critical role in the oxidation–reduction process in the human body due to its special antioxidant feature; however, abnormal GSH content is associated with many diseases, such as hepatic failure, cancer, hyoxemia, and HIV infection [14]. GSH can be produced by yoghurt fermenting by Streptococcus thermophilus strain (S. thermophilus) as the main metabolite of S. thermophilus is GSH and the GSH yield can be evaluated to select the dominant strain of S. thermophiles [15]. Therefore, it is important to develop a highly selective and sensitive method for GSH detection to provide assistance for S. thermophilus strain screening in yoghurt production [16]. Many traditional methods, including nuclear magnetic resonance, HPLC (High Performance Liquid Chromatography), electrochemical, colorimetric have been established to detect GSH content in biological samples [17]. However, most of these methods could not meet the requirement of S. thermophilus strain screening due to the expensive equipment, complicated and time-consuming pretreatment process (including sample collection, protein removal and enzymatic reduction procedure) [18]. Therefore, a new facile, convenient, rapidly method with highly sensitive and selective is urged to establish.

In this work, we prepared nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) via microwave pyrolysis method using fulvic acid, citric acid, and urea as precursors. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed to study the morphology and chemical composition of N-CDs. Then the N-CDs were studied as fluorescent sensor and showed high selectivity and sensitivity fluorescence quenching responses toward Fe3+. Furthermore, the quenched fluorescence of the N-CD/Fe3+ system was recovered in the presence of GSH. On the basis of the quenching and recovering processes, the N-CDs were further used as an “on–off–on” fluorescent sensor to detect GSH and showed excellent performance. The mechanism of the high selectivity and sensitivity were investigated by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Moreover, this N-CDs/Fe3+/GSH sensor was successfully studied in practical application to detect GSH produced by S. thermophilus strain and used as dominant strain screening method in yoghurt production.

Humic acid was purchased from Shandong Chuangxin Humic Acid Technology Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Acetone, sulfuric acid, urea, citric acid, reduced GSH, and inorganic salts (including FeCl3·6H2O, ZnCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, PbCl2, FeCl2·4H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, alanine (Ala), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), histidine (His), phenylalanine (Phe), proline (Pro), tryptophan (Trp) and tyrosine (Tyr) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Company. M − 17 medium was purchased from Qingdao Hope Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). S. thermophilus strain was obtained from China Center of Industrial Culture Collection.

Microwave reactor (MCR-3) was purchased from Gongyi yuhua instrument co. LTD. FT-IR spectra were measured from 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1 on a Nicolet iS 10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo, England). Ultraviolet (UV) absorption spectra were collected on a UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and photoluminescence spectra (PL) were obtained with an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). TEM and HRTEM images were recorded on a JEOL JEM2100 electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra were measured on ESCALABXi+ (Thermo, England). Optical microscopic examination was achieved by using an OLYMPUS CX41TV biological microscope.

Humic acid (100 g), acetone (230 g, 15% water content), and 8 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) were placed in a round-bottom flask under mechanical stirring. The mixture was stirred at 2000 r/min for 4 h at room temperature to obtain a dark brown suspension solution. After collecting the supernatant by centrifugation (10000r/min), the supernatant was dried by freeze drying at −102 °C for 12 h to obtain a yellowish-brown solid with a yield of 23%.

Microwave pyrolysis method was used to synthesize N-CDs. Fulvic acid (0.1 g), urea (1 g), and citric acid (0.1 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and then transferred into a Teflon reactor. The reactor was subjected to microwave-assisted pyrolysis in a microwave reactor at 330 W for 10 min. The obtained dark solution was first diluted in 100 mL of deionized water and then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove large particles. Afterwards, the solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) for 48 h to obtain a dark brown solution of N-CDs. Finally, pure N-CDs were obtained as brown powder by freeze drying at −102 °C for 36 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence selectivity of N-CDs towards Fe3+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: For the selectivity of N-CDs towards Fe3+, different volumes of metal ions (10−3 mol/L) solution were respectively added into 2 mL of 0.2 g/L N-CDs solution, and the volume was adjusted to 5 mL with water. The changes of fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelength at 330 nm were measured to investigate the fluorescence selectivity towards Fe3+ among different kinds of metal ions.

For the selectivity of N-CDs towards GSH, a series of 2 mL of 0.2 g/L N-CDs solution and 3 mL of 10−3 mol/L FeCl3 was placed in glass vials. Then, different concentrations of amino acids (10−2 mol/L) were respectively added into N-CDs/Fe3+ solution. The changes of fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelength at 330 nm were measured to investigate the fluorescence selectivity towards GSH among other amino acids.

The interactions and electronic transition between the N-CDs and various metal ions (Mn+= Fe3+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+and Zn2+) were studied by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Geometry optimizations for the complexes and corresponding monomers were performed by DFT based Becke's three-parameter hybrid exchange functional and Lee–Yang– Parr correlation functional (B3LYP) using 6-31 + G* basis set with the pseudopotentials SDD and Lanl2TZ for the main-group and transition metals respectively. Frequency calculations were done at the same level of theory to ensure all complexes in minima potential energy surfaces. All the electronic structure calculations were performed using GAUSSIAN 09 and Gauss View 5.0 for visualization.

The binding energy (BE) of the complexes were calculated using the supramolecular approach and corrected for basis set superposition error (BSSE) employing the counterpoise (CP) procedure suggested by Boys and Bernardi [19].
(1)
B E =
E
c o m p l e x
−
∑
i = 1
n
E i

As shown in Eq. (1), E
complex is the total energy of the complex, E
i is energy of the monomer (i.e. N-CDs and Mn+ ions). We deduced the energetics with the inclusion of Grimme's (DFT-D3) dispersion correction in order to quantify the role of dispersion energy in these complexes [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of GSH produced by S. thermophilus
ce:section.ce:para: M17 solid medium was prepared by using M17 medium (4.123 g), lactose (0.5 g), and agaropectin (1.5 g), whereas M17 liquid medium was produced without agaropectin. The mixture was first dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and then sterilized at 121 °C for 30 min to obtain the corresponding medium [21]. Afterwards, 0.2 g of N-CDs solution and 0.2 g of N-CDs/Fe3+ solution (v:v = 2:3) were separately mixed with 20 mL of M17 medium to set as the reference samples. Simultaneously, 100 μL of S. thermophilus strain was inoculated into the M17 medium with 0.2 g of N-CDs/Fe3+ solution (v:v = 2:3). Finally, all the samples were incubated at 42 °C for 72 h with anaerobic static culture.

TEM were performed to investigate the morphology and size distribution of the N-CDs. The sample was prepared by dropping the mixture of N-CDs on copper grid. As shown in Fig. 1 a, N-CDs exhibited spherical shape and uniform size distribution. The size distribution of N-CDs ranged from 1.5 nm to 5 nm, and the average diameter was 3.2 nm. The HRTEM image indicated that the synthesized N-CDs had clearly crystalline lattice fringes (0.21 nm) which could be attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene [2]. The functional sites on the surface of N-CDs were measured by FT-IR spectroscopy and shown in Fig. 1c. The peaks located at 3470 and 2072 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of O–H and stretching vibration of N–H. The absorption peaks at 1639 and 1086 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC/CO and C–O [22,23]. These results indicated that abundant –NH2 and –COOH existed on the N-CDs surface.

X-ray photoelectron spectra were measured to further reveal the surface state of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1d, the content ratio of C/N/O for N-CDs was 1/0.33/0.46, which indicated that N-CDs contained some nitrogen [24]. The C 1s of four peaks at 288.55, 286.47, 285.05, and 284.2 eV corresponded to –COOH, C–O, C–N, and CC/C–C, respectively [25]. Three peaks in the N 1s spectrum could be attributed to C–NH2 (399.8 eV), C–N–C (399.16 eV), and CN (398.46 eV) [26]. Furthermore, the two peaks around 531.4 and 530.8 eV in the O 1s spectrum were attributed to C–O–C/C–OH and CO, respectively [27]. The XPS data were consistent with the FT-IR results, which further confirmed that abundant amino and carboxylic groups existed on the surface of the prepared N-CDs.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs were measured and are shown in Fig. 1h. The absorption peaks of N-CDs were centered at 330 nm, which could be attributed to n-π* (originated by CO on the surface of N-CDs) transitions [28]. The emission spectra showed strong emission (quantum yield was 29.4%) with the maximum wavelength at 454 nm under excitation at 330 nm. Moreover, the synthesized N-CDs displayed unique excitation-independent feature for the PL intensities of emission spectra centered at 454 nm showed nearly no shift when the excitation wavelengths changed (Fig. 1i), indicating that the fluorescence emission properties of N-CDs might depended on the surface states rather than the morphology [29].

The pH-sensitive behavior of N-CDs was further measured. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence emission wavelength showed no change with the pH ranging from 3 to 11, while the emission intensity had obvious variations with the changing of pH values. The N-CDs showed the highest intensity at near-neutral pH under the excitation of 330 nm. This pH dependent behavior could be attributed to the reversible protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on N-CD surface. It has been reported that the fluorescent species in the CDs had basic/acidic sites which related to the photoluminescence emission in different media [30]. For N-CDs, the reversible protonation and deprotonation functional groups should be amide and carboxylic groups, which were supported by FT-IR and XPS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
Highly selective is the essential demand for fluorescent sensors [31]. Thus, the fluorescence selectivity between N-CDs and Fe3+ among other metal ions were further investigated. Different metal ions, including Mn2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+, with the same concentration of 10−3 M, were added to the N-CDs solution (0.2 g/L). Fig. 2 a showed that N-CDs exhibited obvious fluorescence quenching towards Fe3+. This unique phenomenon indicated that N-CDs could be developed as an efficient fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ detection [32]. As shown in Fig. 2b, the PL intensity of N-CDs gradually decreased with increasing concentration of Fe3+ ions, which implied that N-CDs had a highly sensitive fluorescence response toward Fe3+ ions. Stern–Volmer plots were utilized to investigate the fluorescence detection sensitivity of N-CDs toward Fe3+. The quenching efficiency was fitted by Stern–Volmer equation [33]:
(2)
1 − I /
I 0
=
K SV
[ M ]
where I 0 represents the initial fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in the absence of Fe3+ ions, I stands for the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions, [M] is the concentration of Fe3+ ions, and K sv is the quenching constant.

Fig. S2a indicated that the Stern–Volmer plot had a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9951) when the concentration of Fe3+ ions increased from 0 μM to 100 μM. This finding implied that N-CDs exhibited satisfactory sensing properties for Fe3+ ion detection. The limit of detection (LoD) was calculated to be 0.102 μM by calculating the signal-to-noise ratio with Eq. (3). This value was much lower than most related reports for Fe3+ detection (Table 1 ) [34].
(3)
LoD = 3
S d
/
K sv
where Sd represents the intensity standard deviation of the blank samples that was measured for 10 times and ksv is the quenching constant of Stern–Volmer plots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical analysis for the selectivity of N-CDs towards metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous reports, the selectivity of N-CDs toward Fe3+ was attributed to the interaction between Fe3+ and NH2 [37,50,51]. Therefore, we selected –NH2 functionalized coronene as a model to study the absorption interaction between N-CDs and metal ions (Mn+= Fe3+, Cr3+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+ and Pb2+) [52]. The optimized geometries of all the surface adsorbed complex N-CDs/Mn+ models were shown in Fig. 4 . It can be clearly seen that –NH2 functionalized coronene model showed a planar structure, and the electron pair on NH2 and the π orbital of coronene formed a big conjugated π orbital, generating interactions with metal ions [53]. With this model, most ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Mg2+ and Zn2+) were absorbed on the surface of N-CDs to generate stable N-CDs/Mn+ complexes. Here, Fe3+ (2.05 Å), Cu2+ (2.00 Å), Co2+ (2.01 Å), Fe2+ (2.03 Å) were strongly bonded with N-CDs, whereas other metal ions were weakly adsorbed on N-CDs surface. Additionally, the corresponding binding energies (BEs), Mulliken charges of metal ions as well as the calculated numbers of charges transfer from N-CDs to metal ions were also calculated and listed in Table 3 . BEs correlated to stability of N-CDs/Mn+ complexes (except for Cr3+), and the stability order of the complexes was Fe3+ > > Cu2+ > Co2+ > Zn2+ > Fe2+ > Mn2+ > Mg2+ > Pb2+ > Ba2+. Obviously, Fe3+ is strongly absorbed on the surface of N-CDs for the calculated BE of N-CDs/Fe3+ is −838.34 kcal/mol, much more higher than other complexes with BEs values ranging from −112.23 to −352.28 kcal/mol. Moreover, the N-CDs/Mn+ complexes with higher BEs are generally accompanied with more charges transfer from N-CDs to metal ions. As shown in Table 3, N-CDs/Fe3+ with the highest BE value (−838.34 kcal/mol) had the largest charge transfer number (2.33), indicating that N-CDs preferred to form stable complex with Fe3+ rather than other metal ions.

To further understand the interactions between N-CDs and metal ions, frontier molecular orbital (FMO) analyses were investigated. The isosurfaces of calculated highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO-1, HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO, LUMO+1, LUMO+2) orbitals of N-CDs/Mn+ were displayed in Fig. 5 . Obviously, the synthesized N-CDs were dominated by π-π* transition, and different kinds of electronic transition were observed when it reacted with Mn+. For the weakly adsorbed N-CDs/Pb2+ and N-CDs/Ba2+, HOMOs were mainly delocalized on the N-CDs surface and the LUMOs localized on the mental ions, leading to less orbital interaction between N-CDs and mental ions. The behavior changed with the increasing of electronic interaction between N-CDs and Mn+. The HOMO-1s were partially transferred to the mental ions for moderately absorbed Mn+ (Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mn2+ and Mg2+), while the transition was transferred from HOMO to Mn+ for strongly absorbed N-CDs/Fe3+. Meanwhile, we found that the LUMOs of the strong absorbed complexes N-CDs/Mn+ (Mn+=Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+ and Zn2+) had obvious π character, while the weak absorbed complexes N-CDs/Mn+ (Mn+=Pb2+ and Ba2+) exhibited σ character. For moderately absorbed complexes N-CDs/Mn+ (Mn+= Fe2+, Mn2+ and Mg2+), these two characters were both observed. It indicated that the interactions between N-CDs and mental ions gradually changed from the weak π-σ* electronic transitions to the strong π-π* electronic transitions along with the enhancement of absorption effects. Combined the geometries and FMO results, it can be concluded that N-CDs preferred to absorb Fe3+ on its surface to form a stable complex rather than other metal ions, which was in good agreement with the fluorescent selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+.

Combined the above experimental and theoretical results, the detecting mechanism of the “on–off–on” sensor was depicted in Fig. 6 . In the “turn off” part, N-CDs acted as the electron-rich fluorescent detector and Fe3+ with half-filled 3 d orbits was the electron deficiency quencher [54]. The mechanism of the “turn off” part could be ascribed to the photoelectron transfer between electron deficiency Fe3+ and N-CDs [11]. As the complex of N-CDs/Fe2+ was not stable when Fe3+ was reduced to Fe2+ by GSH, the complex based on N-CDs and Fe3+ was destroyed. Then the N-CDs were released, and the fluorescence emission was recovered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the “on–off–on” sensor in the GSH-producing capability detection of S. thermophilus
ce:section.ce:para: The S. thermophilus strains are widely used to produce GSH with high yield [55]. Hence, the fluorescent sensor to GSH can be performed as a strain-screening detector to select S. thermophilus strains with high GSH production. Herein, we chose four kinds of S. thermophilus strains (named as SDMCC-1, SDMCC-14, SDMCC-32 and SDMCC-33) to study the performances of the fluorescent sensor towards S. thermophilus strains in both liquid and solid media. For liquid media, all samples were measured after inoculation in L-M17 medium for 72 h. It could be clearly seen in Fig. 7 a that the “on-off-on” sensor displayed the highest recovery degree with SDMCC-32 among these four kinds of strains, indicating SDMCC-32 was the dominant strain for GSH production. Moreover, the fluorescent behaviors of this “on–off–on” sensor towards SDMCC-32 were further investigated in detail. The fluorescence behaviors of N-CDs (marked as 1), N-CDs/Fe3+ “turn off” system (v:v = 2:3, marked as 2), and N-CDs/Fe3+/SDMCC-32 “turn on” system (marked as 3) were measured after inoculation in L-M17 medium for 72 h. As shown in Fig. 7b and c, the quenched fluorescence emission of N-CDs could be effectively recovered by SDMCC-32 and the recovery degree of fluorescence emission in liquid medium was 87.3%. According to the calibration curve for N-CDs/Fe3+ towards GSH, the GSH content was calculated to be 38.95 μM, which was consistent to the result measured by colorimetric method (40.31 μM), suggesting our fluorescent sensor had satisfied accuracy in measurement. Furthermore, the fluorescence recovery of SDMCC-32 in solid medium presenting the similar tendency (Fig. 7d).

The cellular toxicity of N-CDs was further measured to study the biocompatibility of N-CDs. Considering its application in S. thermophiles strain screening, viable count detection method with SDMCC-32 was used after incubating for 24 h, 48 h and 72 h at 42 °C in L-M17 medium with 0%, 5% and 10% content of N-CDs. After incubating for 24 h, the viable counts of the mediums firstly increased to the maximum value and then decreased due to the existing of stress effect within S. thermophiles. According to the results showed in Fig. S3, the bacterial survival rate of the blank, 5% content and 10% content displayed similar tendency, and the values were approximately same. It means the additional of N-CDs have no effect on cellular growth, and this “on–off–on” sensor can be used as strain screening detector in practical application.

In this work, a novel “on–off–on” fluorescent sensor based on N-CDs was developed to detect Fe3+ and GSH. The fluorescence of N-CDs could be effectively quenched by Fe3+ and exhibited high-sensitivity and sensitivity for fluorescence detection towards Fe3+ with an LoD of 0.102 μM, and then recovered by GSH with an LoD of 0.226 μM. Moreover, DFT calculations were performed to study the mechanism of the high sensitivity and sensitivity for fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and GSH. It was found that the synthesized N-CDs exhibited strongest interaction with Fe3+ among all metal ions. FMO results revealed that N-CDs preferred to absorb Fe3+ on its surface and they can form stable complex. The “on–off–on” fluorescence mechanism could be attributed to that the fluorescence emission of N-CDs was quenched by Fe3+ due to photo-induced electron transfer and recovered with GSH on the basis of the redox reaction upon Fe3+. Furthermore, this “on–off–on” fluorescent sensor based on N-CDs showed great potential in practical S. thermophilus strain screening for yoghurt production.
Yan Liang: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Ting Wang: Investigation. Hua Wang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. William W. Yu: Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Project Supported by the Foundation of State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking (No. ZR20190105), National Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (No. 201910431042), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M622197 and 2017M622110), National Natural Science Foundation of China (31701576), Major Science and Technology Innovation Projects of Shandong Province (2018CXGC0212).

The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
